children.name: Machaerirhynchus
children.description: A genus of small, broad-billed passerines endemic to New Guinea and northeastern Queensland, named for their unusually wide, flat bills that are shaped like a boat. They are active insectivores that forage in the forest understorey, catching insects in short sallies from a perch. Their plumage is a combination of yellow, black, and white, making them distinctive among New Guinea's rich avifauna.

children.name: Machaerirhynchus flaviventer
children.description: A small, active bird with a bright yellow breast, black-and-white facial pattern, and a remarkably broad, flattened bill, found in lowland and hill forests of New Guinea and Cape York in Australia. The unusually wide bill helps it catch flying insects in the dim understorey. It is a restless forager, constantly on the move in the lower branches.

children.name: Machaerirhynchus nigripectus
children.description: A montane boatbill with a black breast, a white throat, and a yellow belly, distinguished from its lowland relative by the dark chest, endemic to the highlands of New Guinea. It inhabits montane moss forests from 1,000 to 2,700 metres, where it hawks for insects in the mid-storey. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.
